mappings.rationale: The later Renaissance in Italy and northern Europe, including cultural and intellectual life, is explicitly discussed as a shaping force in this section.
Cultural and intellectual life in the later Renaissance, including Italian artistic achievements and their influence on northern Europe, is covered in this section.
Machiavelli and the principle of raison d'etat are explicitly cited as exemplifying the influence of Italian statecraft and political theory.
The emergence of modern science and technology in the 16th and 17th centuries is explicitly listed as a major development in this section.
The Protestant Reformation and its political and social consequences are a central theme of the section's treatment of 16th-century Europe.
The Catholic Reformation and Counter-Reformation are explicitly discussed as responses to Protestantism in the section.
Martin Luther and the origins of Lutheranism, the Diet and Edict of Worms, and the Peasants' Revolt are explicitly covered in the section.
The emergence of Protestant churches in England, Scotland, France, Germany, and the Netherlands is a major theme throughout this section.
Calvin and the Swiss Reformation, as well as Calvinism's influence in Scotland, France, and the Netherlands, are discussed in the section.
The Counter-Reformation, the Papal States, and Catholic-Protestant conflicts across Europe are central to the section's narrative.
Henry VII, Henry VIII, Edward VI, Mary I, and Elizabeth I are all covered in the section's treatment of the British Isles from c. 1485 to 1603.
James I, Charles I, the English Civil War, Oliver Cromwell, the Commonwealth, and the Glorious Revolution are extensively covered.
The Hanoverian succession, Whig supremacy, ministries of Walpole and Pitt, and the early years of George III's reign are discussed in detail.
The section concludes with the reform movement, the financial crisis, and the States General marking the beginning of the Revolution in 1789.
Habsburg power in Spain, Austria, the Netherlands, and the Holy Roman Empire is a central organizing theme, from Charles V through Maria Theresa.
Germany and the Holy Roman Empire from c. 1500 to 1648, including the Reformation, Counter-Reformation, and Thirty Years' War, are extensively covered.
European overseas expansion, establishment of colonial empires, and the impact of colonial expansion on Europe are major themes of the section.
The reform movement, rationalism, and intellectual developments in 18th-century France and Britain leading up to the Revolution connect to the Enlightenment.
Voyages of discovery and exploration, advances in geographical knowledge, and the establishment of colonial empires are explicitly discussed.
Advances in navigational instruments, ship design, and cartography that enabled European overseas expansion are explicitly mentioned.
The English Civil War and the political upheaval of the 17th century provide the context for Hobbes's political philosophy.
The Glorious Revolution of 1688, limited monarchy, and the growth of political parties are the context for Locke's political philosophy.
The reform movement in France, the influences of nationalism and individualism, and the prelude to the French Revolution connect to Rousseau's thought.
The Dutch Republic's cultural, social, and religious developments in the 17th century provide the context for Spinoza's philosophy.
Edinburgh's status as an intellectual centre and the Scottish Enlightenment are the backdrop for Hume's philosophical contributions.
The Scientific Revolution and the emergence of modern science and technology in the 17th century provide the context for Descartes' rationalism.
The French ancien regime, the reform movement, and attacks on political and social policies connect to Voltaire's role as a leading Enlightenment critic.
The French Wars of Religion and the cultural developments of 16th-century France provide the context for Montaigne's essays.
The Scientific Revolution and the emergence of modern science in the 16th and 17th centuries directly involve Galileo's contributions.
The Scientific Revolution and the emergence of modern science continue through Newton's work in the late 17th century.
The Scientific Revolution's emergence in the 16th century was inaugurated by Copernicus's heliocentric theory.
Shakespeare's work is central to the cultural developments in Elizabethan and Jacobean England covered in this section.
The growth of representative institutions, from the English Parliament to the Dutch Republic, and the beginning of parliamentary reform movements are discussed.
The persecution of Puritans under Charles I, the role of Puritanism in the English Civil War, and Cromwell's Commonwealth are covered.
The decline of feudalism, growth of commercial activity, joint-stock companies, and mercantilist policies are central economic themes of this section.
The 'price revolution,' growth of mercantilism, increase in world trade volume, and development of the slave trade connect to global economic developments.
The section's coverage extends to the origins of the French Revolution, setting the stage for the Napoleonic era that immediately followed.
The development and importance of the slave trade, including the growth of the agricultural trade in tobacco, sugar, and coffee, are explicitly discussed.
The conflict between the nobility and bourgeoisie, the decline of feudalism, and the role of aristocratic power across European states are pervasive themes.
Ireland's subjugation by Henry VIII and Elizabeth I, the Irish revolt of 1641, Cromwell's invasion, and the growth of Irish patriotism are discussed.
The Protestant Ascendancy in Ireland and the origins of Anglo-Irish tensions covered in the section provide historical roots of Northern Ireland's conflicts.
Scotland in the 16th and 17th centuries, the Scottish Reformation, John Knox, and the 1707 union are explicitly covered.
Scandinavia's emergence from the medieval period into the early modern era, including the separation of Sweden from Denmark-Norway, connects to the Viking legacy.
The establishment of the Church of England under Henry VIII and subsequent religious developments under Elizabeth I are explicitly discussed.
The origins of the Industrial Revolution and factory system, pre-industrial manufacturing, and agricultural innovations are discussed as emerging at the end of this period.
The religiously divided Europe of the 16th and 17th centuries and the Counter-Reformation created the context for early modern witch trials.
The influence of Methodism on 18th-century Britain is explicitly mentioned as part of the section's discussion of economic, cultural, and social developments.
International diplomacy and warfare from 1494 to 1763, including the balance of power concept and the alliance system, are major themes.
Italy's political, economic, and social developments from the French invasion of 1494 through the 18th century under various foreign dominations are covered.
Spain's Golden Age cultural developments during the reigns of Philip II through Philip IV are discussed in the section.
French cultural development in the 17th century and the intellectual life of the ancien regime are part of the broader literary and cultural history.
The Reformation's intellectual legacy in Germany and the broader philosophical developments of the early modern period connect to German philosophical traditions.
The conflicts between crown and Parliament in the Stuart period and the Glorious Revolution extend the legacy of Magna Carta's limitations on royal power.
From Machiavelli through Locke and Rousseau, the section traces the evolution of political theory across early modern Europe.
The growth of individualism, parliamentary reform, and challenges to absolute monarchy in the 17th and 18th centuries represent early liberal developments.
The cultural developments at the end of this period, particularly the reform movements and the influence of Rousseau, set the stage for Romanticism.
The section is organized around dynastic states -- the Habsburgs, Bourbons, Stuarts, Vasas, and Houses of Orange and Hanover.
German cultural and intellectual developments in the 18th century, emerging from the post-Westphalian landscape, provide the context for Goethe's work.
The Norman Conquest's legacy in creating the English constitutional traditions that later clashed with Stuart absolutism provides deep historical context.
The balance of power system, colonial rivalries, and the emergence of nation-states from c. 1500 to 1789 are fundamentally geopolitical developments.
The emergence of the nation-state system, the Peace of Westphalia, and the development of the alliance system shaped the modern international order.
The growth of mercantilism, commercial development, and the origins of the Industrial Revolution provide the context for Adam Smith's economic thought.
The development and importance of the slave trade in European colonial expansion eventually gave rise to the abolitionist movements emerging at the end of this period.
Thomas More was a key Tudor-era statesman and author of Utopia, directly relevant to this section's coverage of Western Europe from 1500 to 1789 including the Reformation.
